summary: Polish filmmaker known for the TV miniseries Dekalog (1988), The Double Life of Veronique (1991), and the Three Colours trilogy (Blue, White, Red — 1993–94). The Three Colours films are themed on the principles of the French tricolor: liberty, equality, fraternity. His regular composer was Zbigniew Preisner. Died while writing a trilogy inspired by Dante's Divine Comedy.

links.text: The Double Life of Véronique — Wikipedia

works.name: Three Colours: Blue (1993)
works.description: The first film of the Three Colours trilogy, themed on liberty. Julie (Juliette Binoche) loses her husband — the composer Patrice de Courcy — and their daughter in a car accident in the opening. Julie tries to sever herself from all attachments. Scored by Zbigniew Preisner, including the 'Song for the Unification of Europe.' Wikipedia

clues.clue: Opens with a car accident killing the composer Patrice de Courcy and his daughter, leaving Juliette Binoche's Julie to grieve
examples: The first of those films by this man opens with a car accident killing the composer Patrice de Courcy and his daughter, leaving Juliette Binoche's Julie to grieve for them.
One film by this director begins with a car accident that kills the composer Patrice de Courcy and his daughter but leaves his wife Julie alive.

clues.clue: A tear rolling down Julie's eye ends a montage scored with Preisner's 'Song for the Unification of Europe'
examples: In one of this man's films, a tear rolling down a widow's eye ends a montage that is scored with the 'Song for the Unification of Europe' by this man's regular collaborator Zbigniew Preisner.

clues.clue: Derek Jarman's final film consisted of a single shot of the color blue; this film shares the color title
examples: Jarman's final feature film consists of a single shot of this color, which also names the first film in Krzysztof Kieslowski's Three Colours trilogy.

clues.clue: Themed on liberty — the first principle of the French revolutionary motto
examples: A director from one of these two countries used a film about a composer's grieving widow to begin a series themed on the other's flag.

works.name: Three Colours: White (1994)
works.description: The second film in the trilogy, themed on equality. A Polish hairdresser, Karol, smuggles himself in a suitcase from France back to Poland to seek revenge on his ex-wife (Julie Delpy), who divorced him for impotence. Wikipedia

examples: In another film, a seemingly-irrelevant shot of a suitcase at an airport is revealed to contain a man smuggling himself from one of these countries to the other.

clues.clue: A protagonist stumbles upon two hairdressers (from Poland and France) divorcing due to the husband's impotence
examples: En route to meeting a lawyer, a film's protagonist stumbles upon two hairdressers from these two countries divorcing due to the husband's impotence.

works.name: Three Colours: Red (1994)
works.description: The final film of the trilogy, themed on fraternity. Irene Jacob plays Valentine, a model in Geneva. An old judge spies via telephone on his neighbors. Ends with a ferry accident on the English Channel. Features the fictional composer Van den Budenmayer, created by Kieslowski and Preisner. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Ends with seven of the main characters surviving a ferry accident on the English Channel; the final shot replicates Valentine's chewing gum poster (Irene Jacob)
examples: A series of films directed by this man ends after seven of the main characters survive a ferry accident on a shot replicating the Irene Jacob-played Valentine's chewing gum poster.
In this film's final scene, an old judge who spies via phone on his neighbors in Geneva learns that a storm has wrecked a ferry on the English Channel.

works.name: The Double Life of Véronique (1991)
works.description: A 1991 film in which Irene Jacob plays two women with parallel lives: a Polish soprano and a French music teacher, both named Veronique. The film explores themes of doubles and intuition. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Irene Jacob plays both a Polish soprano and a French music teacher, both named Veronique, who have parallel lives
examples: A 1991 film by this director examines the parallel lives of a Polish soprano and a French music teacher, both named Veronique.
Two women with seemingly unconnected lives, a singer in Poland and a music teacher in France, are both played by Irene Jacob in this 1991 film by Krzysztof Kieslowski.

examples: In a film set in these two countries, a green-tinged sequence depicts a teacher watching a marionette show.

works.name: Dekalog / The Decalogue (1988 TV)
works.description: A ten-episode TV miniseries set in a late-Communist Warsaw housing project, each episode loosely inspired by one of the Ten Commandments. An unnamed witness character (Artur Barcis) appears in almost every episode. Two episodes were expanded into A Short Film About Killing and A Short Film About Love. Wikipedia

comprehensive_summary:
Krzysztof Kieślowski (1941–1996) was a Polish filmmaker best known for the TV miniseries Dekalog (1988) and the Three Colours trilogy (Blue, White, Red — 1993–94). His regular composer was Zbigniew Preisner, and together they created the fictional Dutch composer Van den Budenmayer, who appears in several of their works. At the time of his death, Kieślowski was writing a trilogy of scripts inspired by Dante's Divine Comedy.

The Three Colours trilogy is themed on the three principles of the French revolutionary motto (and the colors of the French flag): liberty (Blue), equality (White), and fraternity (Red). Three Colours: Blue (1993) opens with a car accident that kills composer Patrice de Courcy and his daughter, leaving Juliette Binoche's character Julie to grieve. The film ends with a tear rolling down Julie's eye during a montage scored with Preisner's 'Song for the Unification of Europe.'

Three Colours: White (1994) follows a Polish hairdresser, Karol, who smuggles himself in a suitcase from France back to Poland to exact revenge on his ex-wife (Julie Delpy), who divorced him for impotence. A protagonist stumbles upon two hairdressers from Poland and France who are divorcing for the same reason.

Three Colours: Red (1994) stars Irene Jacob as Valentine, a model in Geneva who befriends an elderly judge who secretly spies via telephone on his neighbors. A fast-paced montage dollying along phone lines opens the film. Both male protagonists gaze at Valentine's profile on a chewing gum poster while driving. A shot at a bowling alley pans to a broken glass, implying a man's rage at infidelity. The final scene has a storm wrecking a ferry on the English Channel, leaving seven characters from the trilogy to survive. The final shot replicates Valentine's chewing gum poster. Red was Kieślowski's last mention of Van den Budenmayer.

The Double Life of Véronique (1991) has Irene Jacob playing both a Polish soprano and a French music teacher, both named Veronique, whose lives run parallel. A green-tinged sequence shows the music teacher watching a marionette show. The camera pans from a man taking off headphones in a store to the protagonist listening to the same song.

Dekalog (1988) is a ten-episode TV miniseries set in a late-Communist Warsaw housing project, each episode loosely inspired by one of the Ten Commandments. An unnamed witness character (Artur Barcis) appears in all but two installments. The first episode involves a boy drowning in a skating accident despite computer calculations showing the ice was thick enough; a subplot about sour milk introduces a recurring motif. In the second episode, a dying man is revived when a bee crawls out of a glass of strawberry syrup. Two episodes were expanded into A Short Film About Killing and A Short Film About Love — the latter follows a post office worker who spies on an older woman.

Film: Irene Jacob plays both a Polish soprano and a French music teacher, both named Veronique, whose lives run parallel; a green-tinged sequence shows the teacher watching a marionette show
